era: Extreme storm event. Pacific atmospheric river or bomb cyclone striking the full Cascade Range simultaneously. The SPS absolute ceiling first reached in the summit.
ecosystem: Full Cascade Range (alpine summit through subalpine through forested slopes through river valleys to Puget Sound)
frequency_range: Sub-Hz (infrasound) through broadband atmospheric noise (20 kHz)

theory_nodes.concept: Cascades Full Storm: not a single weather event at one elevation but the simultaneous full acoustic expression of a major Pacific atmospheric river or bomb cyclone across the entire Cascade Range vertical profile. Mount Rainier summit (14,411 ft): 100+ mph winds, blizzard, extreme cold. Alpine zone (8,000-12,000 ft): full blizzard, massive snowfall, wind-driven snow creating white-noise acoustic environment. Subalpine zone (4,000-8,000 ft): mixed precipitation, forest canopy in full motion. Forested slopes (1,000-4,000 ft): old-growth Douglas fir canopy in gale, each tree a sound source. River valleys (below 1,000 ft): flooding rivers at roar, boulders moving in flood. Puget Sound: storm surge, breaking waves, wind-driven chop. All simultaneously.
theory_nodes.description: The Cascades Full Storm -- entire vertical profile from summit to sea in extreme motion.

theory_nodes.concept: Mount Rainier at 14,411 feet: the highest point in the Cascade Range and one of the most extreme weather environments in North America. The summit receives among the highest wind speeds recorded on land (recorded gusts of 160+ mph during extreme events). The acoustic environment at the summit during a full storm is: continuous broadband wind roar (white noise, 20-20,000 Hz), structural vibration of rock and ice formations, the infrasound generated by katabatic wind flows down the mountain's flanks. The summit itself vocalizes during a full storm.
theory_nodes.description: Mount Rainier summit acoustics during extreme storm.

theory_nodes.concept: The old-growth forest in full wind: Cascade Range old-growth Douglas fir, western red cedar, and Sitka spruce can exceed 250 feet in height. During a gale, each tree becomes a sound source: the creak and groan of the trunk under load, the rush of wind through the canopy (which sounds different from young forest -- the old-growth canopy is complex, multi-layered, with competing surfaces creating broadband turbulence noise), the crack of breaking branches, the concussion of fallen trees. A Cascade old-growth stand in full storm is one of the most acoustically complex natural environments in the region.
theory_nodes.description: Old-growth forest in full storm -- each tree a complex sound source.

theory_nodes.concept: River flooding acoustics: Cascade rivers in flood are qualitatively different from normal flow. The volume of water creates a continuous broadband roar in the 50-500 Hz range. More distinctively, boulders and large rocks are moved by floodwater -- the sound of a 100-pound boulder rolling along a riverbed is a deep, irregular percussion heard as a low thud or knock. This is not a common sound; it is audible only during flood events when the water velocity exceeds the threshold for boulder transport. The acoustic signature of boulder movement is the aural marker of true river flooding.
theory_nodes.description: Flood acoustics -- from roar to boulder movement.

theory_nodes.concept: E=10 calibration: the Cascades Full Storm is the SPS absolute maximum on the physical/meteorological dimension. No single weather event at one location matches it; the power is in the simultaneous expression across the full 14,000-foot vertical range. The horizontal extent (the Cascade Range runs 700 miles from British Columbia to Northern California) means the storm's acoustic output is generated across an enormous area simultaneously. E=10 = all dimensions at maximum: horizontal extent, vertical range, intensity at each elevation, duration (atmospheric rivers can persist 24-72 hours).
theory_nodes.description: Why Cascades Full Storm is E=10 -- simultaneous maximum across all dimensions.

theory_nodes.concept: Summit-to-sea thematic mirror with Superunknown: 'Let Me Drown' (Superunknown opener) to 'Like Suicide' (Superunknown closer) is a 70-minute arc from arrival to descent. The Cascades storm arc runs from summit (Rainier, 14,411 ft, extreme altitude, maximum wind) through the vertical range to sea level (Puget Sound surge, wave action). Both the album and the storm move from maximum altitude/intensity to the sound of water. The thematic mirroring is structural: both cover the full range from summit to depth within a single continuous event.
theory_nodes.description: Summit-to-sea thematic mirror between Superunknown and Cascades Full Storm.

cross_references.relationship: E=10 + E=10 = E=20. The summit's second E=20. Superunknown's vertical arc (summit to depth) mirrors the Cascades storm's vertical arc (summit to sea). Both cover the full range from maximum to ocean level within a single 70-minute event.
E=9 rises to E=10. Pacific infrasound is the acoustic foundation; Cascades Full Storm is the mountain expression of the same Pacific energy system driving ashore and ascending the range.
Consecutive E=10 entries. Cascades Full Storm is the meteorological E=10 maximum; Cougar/Wolf/Bear is the biological E=10 maximum in the same wilderness space.

sps_pair.pairing_note: E=10 + E=10 = E=20. Summit-to-sea mirror structure. Superunknown's arc from 'Let Me Drown' to 'Like Suicide' (70 minutes, summit to depth) mirrors the Cascades storm arc (Rainier summit 14,411 ft to Puget Sound surge). The most structurally coherent E=20 pairing in the summit.

summit_arc: 345 Pacific Infrasound E=9 → 346 Cascades Full Storm E=10 (FIRST SPS CEILING) → 347 Cougar/Wolf/Bear E=10 → 348 Full SRKW Greeting E=10.
